title: Onion Gravy
subtitle: Slowly caramelised onions, stock, and a splash of Worcestershire
excerpt: Onion gravy starts with onions cooked very slowly until deep golden-brown and sweet, not the pale, lightly softened onions of a quick weeknight sauce. That low-and-slow step is what gives the gravy its colour and depth. Beef stock, a splash of Worcestershire sauce, and a little flour to thicken it complete the picture. It keeps well and improves the next day.
sourceNotes: Classic British gravy for roast meats, sausages, and toad-in-the-hole. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: onion sauce and brown gravy. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): onion gravy method. Standard British home cooking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Reheat gently, adding a splash of water if too thick.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make a large batch and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead and store in the fridge. Reheat gently over a low heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking sliced onions over a low-to-medium heat for 30 to 40 minutes until the natural sugars convert to caramel compounds. The onions become soft, deeply golden-brown, and sweet. This is a slow process: high heat burns the outside before the sugars develop.

content.text: The quality of onion gravy depends almost entirely on how long the onions cook. Rushing the

produces a pale, slightly sharp gravy without depth. The onions need 30 to 40 minutes over a medium-low heat, stirring regularly, until they are a deep tawny-brown and smell sweet. There is no shortcut. Adding a pinch of sugar can help the browning slightly, but time is what the onions need.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onions with a pinch of {{sea-salt-flakes}} and stir to coat. Cook over a low heat for 30 to 40 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes, until the onions are very soft and deep golden-brown. Do not rush this step.

Stir {{plain-flour}} of flour into the onions and cook for 1 minute, stirring. Gradually pour in the hot {{stock-beef}}, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Add {{worcestershire-sauce}}, {{black-pepper}}, and remaining {{sea-salt-flakes}}. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 10 minutes until the gravy has thickened. Taste and adjust seasoning.

rows.fix: There is no fixing pale gravy after the fact. Next time, cook the onions for the full 40 minutes. A little Marmite or dark soy sauce stirred in can add colour to an under-cooked batch.

rows.cause: Flour not fully incorporated or stock added too quickly
rows.fix: Whisk vigorously and strain through a fine sieve. Add stock gradually in future.

content.text: Onion gravy is the standard gravy for toad-in-the-hole, sausages, and liver and onions. It also works alongside roast beef and lamb, though the dripping-based gravy from the roasting pan is the more traditional choice for a Sunday joint. This version is the one made when there are no roasting pan juices to work with.
